book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
The Sixth Passage of Scripture Quoted by Victor.
originalBook: Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
title: The Sixth Passage of Scripture Quoted by Victor.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Chapter 29 [XVIII.]—The Sixth Passage of Scripture Quoted by Victor.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Let us now look at the quotation from Genesis, where the woman was created out of the side of the man, and was brought to him, and he said: “This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Our opponent thinks that “Adam ought to have said, ‘Soul of my soul, or spirit of my spirit,’ if this, too, had been derived from him.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But, in fact, they who maintain the opinion of the propagation of souls feel that they possess a more impregnable defence of their position in the fact that in the Scripture narrative which informs us that God took a rib out of the man’s side and formed it into a woman, it is not added that He breathed into her face the breath of life; for this reason, as they say, because she had already been ensouled from the man.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If, indeed, she had not, they say, the sacred Scripture would certainly not have kept us in ignorance of the circumstance.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: With regard to the fact that Adam says, “This is now bone of my bone, and flesh of my flesh,” without adding, Spirit or soul, from my spirit or soul, they may answer, just as it has been already shown, that the expression, “my flesh and bone,” may be understood as indicating the whole by a part, only that the portion that was taken out of man was not dead, but ensouled; for no good ground for denying that the Almighty was able to do all this is furnished by the circumstance that not a human being could be found capable of cutting off a part of a man’s flesh along with the soul.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Adam went on, however, to say, “She shall be called woman, because she was taken out of man.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, why does he not rather say (and thus confirm the opinion of our opponents), “Since her flesh was taken out of man”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: As the case stands, indeed, they who hold the opposite view may well contend, from the fact that it is written, not woman’s flesh, but the woman herself was taken out of man, that she must be considered in her entire nature endued with soul and spirit.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For although the soul is undistinguished by sex, yet when women are mentioned it is not necessary to regard them apart from the soul.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: On no other principle would they be thus admonished with respect to self-adornment.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: “Not with braided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array; but which (says the apostle) becometh women professing godliness with a good conversation.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, “godliness,” of course, is an inner principle in the soul or spirit; and yet they are called women, although the ornamentation concerns that internal portion of their nature which has no sex.
